scholarship_request_details.reason_you_are_requesting_this_scholarsh:
I am requesting this National Testing Network fee scholarship because the $49 testing fee is a significant barrier for me right now given my current financial situation and responsibilities. I want to be very transparent and detailed so you understand where I’m coming from.

I am 27 years old and currently working part-time as a security officer at a grocery distribution warehouse in Portland while also taking occasional community college classes when I can afford them. My hours fluctuate between 24–32 hours a week depending on staffing needs, and I earn $19.25 per hour. After taxes, I typically bring home between $1,600 and $1,900 per month. Out of that, my share of rent for my apartment is $875 per month, plus around $110 for my portion of utilities and internet. I also have a used 2012 Honda Civic that I need to get to and from work on swing and graveyard shifts, and that costs me about $210 a month in insurance and an average of $120 in gas. I am also still paying off a small amount of community college debt (about $45 per month on a payment plan).

On top of my own bills, I regularly help my mom, who lives in Gresham, with groceries and her phone bill. She works as a caregiver and does not have a lot of financial flexibility, and there have been multiple months where I’ve had to choose between putting a little aside for savings or making sure she has enough food and can keep her phone on. I usually send her between $75 and $100 a month. That doesn’t sound like a lot on paper, but with my income and expenses, it makes a big difference.

When I look at my budget at the end of each month, I typically have less than $50 left over, and that’s assuming nothing unexpected happens—no car repairs, no medical co-pays, no surprise charges. For example, last month I had to replace a tire and get my brakes checked, which wiped out what little cushion I had. Because of that, paying an additional $49 for the NTN test fee would mean either not paying a bill on time or skipping groceries or gas for a week. I’ve already cut back on non-essentials like eating out and entertainment; I don’t have any subscriptions other than a basic phone plan and shared internet.

I’m extremely motivated to apply to become a Community Police Officer with the Portland Police Bureau because I grew up in East Portland and have seen both the challenges and the potential in our neighborhoods. I’ve had both positive and negative interactions with law enforcement over the years, and those experiences have made me want to be part of the solution—someone who can communicate calmly, de-escalate tense situations, and build trust with people who might not feel heard. In my current security job, I’ve already had to handle situations involving shoplifting, disagreements between co-workers, and individuals in mental health crises. In those moments, I’ve learned how important it is to stay patient, listen carefully, and treat everyone with dignity, even when they are upset or scared.

I don’t want the application or testing process to be delayed just because of the fee. I’ve already taken the time to review the minimum requirements, adjust my schedule around potential testing dates, and talk with my supervisor about being flexible with shifts so I can move forward in the hiring process. The scholarship would allow me to take the NTN test as soon as possible instead of waiting several months to save up the extra $49. Given how tight my budget is, waiting to save that amount realistically means postponing my application, which I really don’t want to do because I feel ready to commit to this path now.

In short, I am not asking for the scholarship because I don’t value the process or because I am unwilling to invest in my future. I am asking because, at this moment in my life, every dollar is accounted for, and an extra $49 would create real strain on my ability to pay for essentials and to continue supporting my mom. Receiving this scholarship would remove that financial barrier and allow me to focus fully on preparing for the test and presenting myself as a strong candidate for the Community Police Officer position.
